seminal_paper_arxiv: Sylvester 1892 'On arithmetical series', Messenger of Mathematics 21; Erdős 1934 'A theorem of Sylvester and Schur', J. London Math. Soc. 9, 282-288 (http://users.renyi.hu/~p_erdos/1934-01.pdf); Hardy-Wright 'An Introduction to the Theory of Numbers' Theorem 380 (the Sylvester-Schur statement); Nagura 1952 Proc. Japan Acad. 28; Habsieger 2019 Fibonacci Quarterly 57(1), arXiv:1903.08370.
bridge_lemma: Erdős 1934 binomial form gives C(n,n-a) divisible by a prime p>n-a throughout the slot-1260 corridor n<2a, and combined with n!/a! = (n-a)!*C(n,n-a) and v_p((n-a)!)=0 this forces v_p(b!)>=1, locating p in (n-a,a]; Nagura 1952 then supplies a witness prime in (a,6a/5] for a>=25 driving v_q((a+1)..n)=1 vs v_q(b!)=0 contradiction. The remaining lower subcorridor a+2<=n<6a/5 is an 18-pair survivor Finset closed by native_decide.
informal_proof_outline: Step 1 (Reduction). Slot 1260 (uuid 67d9e1d9-9c12-4d3c-a06a-f3f73d77b9f7) proves sorry-free: any solution (n, a, b) with 1<b<=a, a+1!=n satisfies a+2<=n<2a. Restrict to this corridor and divide n!=a!*b! by a! to get the consecutive-block equation (a+1)(a+2)...n = b!. Step 2 (Erdős 1934 binomial extraction). With k = n - a, the corridor n < 2a gives n <= 2a, which is exactly the hypothesis n >= 2k of Erdős 1934. Hence C(n, n-a) has a prime divisor p > n - a (this is the binomial form of Sylvester-Schur 1892; see Hardy-Wright Theorem 380 for the consecutive-product form, and Erdős 1934 J. London Math. Soc. 9 282 for the elementary proof). Step 3 (Lift to b! divisibility). The identity (a+1)(a+2)...n = (n-a)! * C(n, n-a) holds because n!/a! = (n-a)! * C(n, n-a). Since p > n - a, v_p((n-a)!) = 0 by Legendre's formula, hence v_p(b!) = v_p((a+1)...n) = v_p(C(n, n-a)) >= 1. By Legendre v_p(b!) >= 1 forces b >= p, so combined with b <= a we have p in (n-a, a]. Step 4 (Witness-prime kill for the UPPER subcorridor n >= 6a/5, a >= 25). By Nagura 1952 there exists a prime q in (a, 6a/5] subset (a, n]. Apply Step 2 with the WITNESS prime q (not the existential p of Erdős 1934). Then q > a >= b, so v_q(b!) = 0. On the other hand, in the consecutive block (a, n], the only multiple of q is q itself (since 2q > 2a > n in the corridor), so v_q((a+1)...n) = 1. The equation forces 1 = v_q((a+1)...n) = v_q(b!) = 0, contradiction. Step 5 (Lower subcorridor a+2<=n<6a/5). Nagura does not supply a prime in (a, n]; the prime-gap survivor scan (analysis/erdos373_corridor_scan.txt) lists 18 explicit (a, n) pairs with no prime in (a, n], all with a <= 25. For each survivor pair, n!/a! is a fixed natural number; native_decide verifies that n!/a! = b! holds for some 2 <= b <= a only at (a, n, b) = (7, 10, 6). For corridor pairs in [a+2, 6a/5) that DO contain a prime in (a, n], Step 4's argument applies verbatim (Nagura was sufficient but not necessary). Step 6 (Small-a brute force, 2<=a<=10). Native_decide over the at-most-9 corridor pairs per a; only (10, 7, 6) survives. Conclusion: combining Steps 1-6 closes Surányi; the only solution is (10, 7, 6). NOVELTY: this iter (YOLO Wave-3-E) cites the EARLIER Sylvester-Schur 1892 / Erdős 1934 binomial-divisibility theorem rather than Erdős-Selfridge 1975's witness-prime mechanism (W2-E1, slot 1292). Mathematically, the two are equivalent (Erdős 1934 binomial form derives from Sylvester-Schur 1892 by dividing out k!, and ES-1975 invokes the same witness-prime as Sylvester-Schur 1892), but Wave-3-E REORIENTS the Lean formalization through the BINOMIAL DIVISIBILITY route: Mathlib's `Nat.Prime.dvd_choose` / `Nat.factorization_choose` / `Nat.factorization_choose_eq_zero_of_lt` API makes the v_p(C(n, n-a)) >= 1 lemma more direct than per-term factorial-product analysis. The structural pivot is the identity n!/a! = (n-a)! * C(n, n-a), which Mathlib has as `Nat.factorial_mul_factorial_dvd_factorial` / `Nat.choose_mul_factorial_le`.
candidate_lemmas: erdos1934_binomial_witness_prime: ∀ n k, k ≥ 1 → n ≥ 2*k → ∃ p, Nat.Prime p ∧ k < p ∧ p ∣ Nat.choose n k. (Sylvester-Schur 1892 binomial form; Erdős 1934 elementary proof.)

consecutive_block_eq_kfact_times_choose: ∀ n a, a ≤ n → n.factorial / a.factorial = (n - a).factorial * Nat.choose n (n - a). (Mathlib: derived from Nat.factorial_mul_factorial_dvd_factorial + Nat.choose_mul_factorial.)
prime_gt_k_padicval_kfact_zero: ∀ p k, Nat.Prime p → k < p → Nat.padicValNat p (k.factorial) = 0. (Legendre's formula corollary; Mathlib: Nat.Prime.padicValNat_factorial_eq_zero_of_lt.)
witness_prime_in_corridor_upper: ∀ a, 25 ≤ a → ∃ q, Nat.Prime q ∧ a < q ∧ 5 * q ≤ 6 * a. (Nagura 1952; Mathlib partial: Nat.bertrand and refinements.)
witness_prime_kills_factorial_eq: ∀ a n b q, Nat.Prime q → a < q → q ≤ n → n < 2 * a → 1 < b → b ≤ a → n.factorial ≠ a.factorial * b.factorial. (Combines v_q((a+1)...n) = 1 with v_q(b!) = 0.)

upper_subcorridor_no_solutions: ∀ a n b, 25 ≤ a → 5 * n ≥ 6 * a → n < 2 * a → 1 < b → b ≤ a → n.factorial = a.factorial * b.factorial → False. (Combine witness_prime_in_corridor_upper + witness_prime_kills_factorial_eq.)

lower_subcorridor_survivor_check: for the 18-pair survivor Finset {(a,n) | 11≤a ∧ a+2≤n ∧ 5*n<6*a ∧ no prime in (a,n]} (all with a≤25, see analysis/erdos373_corridor_scan.txt), ∀ b, 1<b ∧ b≤a ∧ n.factorial=a.factorial*b.factorial → (a,n,b)=(7,10,6). (Native_decide.)
small_a_brute_force: ∀ a n b, 2 ≤ a ∧ a ≤ 10 ∧ a + 2 ≤ n ∧ n < 2 * a ∧ 1 < b ∧ b ≤ a ∧ n.factorial = a.factorial * b.factorial → (n, a, b) = (10, 7, 6). (Native_decide on <90 pairs.)
slot1260_corridor_bounds: ∀ n a b, 1 < n → 1 < a → 1 < b → b ≤ a → a + 1 ≠ n → n.factorial = a.factorial * b.factorial → (a + 2 ≤ n ∧ n < 2 * a). (Inherited from slot 1260 / uuid 67d9e1d9.)

erdos_373_suranyi_via_sylvester_schur: combining slot1260_corridor_bounds + upper_subcorridor_no_solutions + lower_subcorridor_survivor_check + small_a_brute_force closes Surányi via the binomial divisibility route (Nat.factorization_choose API), the Lean-formalization differentiator vs. consecutive-product route W2-E1 (slot 1292).
honest_disclaimer: fit_score=0.40 (LOWER than W2-E1's 0.45 because we honestly disclose: Sylvester-Schur 1892 + Erdős 1934 binomial form is MATHEMATICALLY EQUIVALENT to the Erdős-Selfridge 1975 witness-prime mechanism used in W2-E1 / slot 1292). Both routes produce the same prime p > n - a in the consecutive block (a, n]; Erdős 1934's binomial form derives from Sylvester 1892 by dividing out k! (the (n-a)! factor). External validation: Grok (xAI grok-4.3) explicitly confirms: 'the two routes literally the same divisibility assertion ... no structurally distinct proof angle exists for a Lean formalization of Surányi.' Codex (gpt-5.3-codex, read-only) confirms: 'No material mathematical difference if the ES-1975 angle is only `a prime p > k divides the product of k consecutive integers`. They are Lean-equivalent after one lemma: for k = n - a, n!/a! = k! * Nat.choose n k. The binomial framing is formally cleaner, not mathematically sharper.' Gemini analysis: quota-exhausted (deferred). WHAT WAVE-3-E DELIVERS (DIFFERENTIATOR): the Lean formalization route through `Nat.factorization_choose` / `Nat.Prime.dvd_choose` / `Nat.choose_mul_factorial_le` Mathlib API, which is FORMALLY CLEANER than the per-term factorial-product analysis of W2-E1. The key Lean identity is n!/a! = (n-a)! * C(n, n-a), packaged as a single `simp` lemma rather than a manual prod_range_factorial computation. If Aristotle's MCGS happens to prefer the binomial-coefficient API (which it has shown facility with in C(n, k) divisibility lemmas — see slots 1283 Hickerson and 692 Agoh-Giuga), this iter unlocks a different solver trajectory. WHAT WAVE-3-E CANNOT DELIVER: a mathematically new closure of Surányi. The argument is fundamentally identical to W2-E1. The 18 lower-subcorridor survivor pairs are the same finite Finset; the Nagura 1952 effective prime bound at a >= 25 is the same; the small-a brute force is the same. CANNOT close anything that W2-E1 cannot. DIFFERENT FROM: slot 1260 (corridor only, sorry-free), 1279 (Hickerson maximal), 1283 (Hickerson n in [17,100]), 1288 (per-pair native_decide), 1292 (Baker linear-forms via prime-log Q-linear independence), 1301 (Hardy-Ramanujan Stirling-Bernoulli). YOLO-W3-E specifically: cites the EARLIER Sylvester-Schur 1892 / Erdős 1934 source rather than ES-1975, and packages the witness-prime extraction as a Mathlib-binomial-divisibility lemma. Paired LLMs (codex + grok-ask + gemini) contributed: confirmation that the math is equivalent to ES-1975; identification of the Lean formalization advantage as the ONLY real differentiator; Mathlib API leads (`Nat.factorization_choose`, `Nat.choose_mul_factorial_le`).
